title: Lattice polarons with extended interactions
authors: Enrique I. Ramírez-Juárez, Genaro Lopez-Olivera, Luis A. Peña Ardila, Arturo Camacho-Guardian
abstract: Lattice impurities have recently emerged as a platform in which polarons unveil new quantum many-body states absent in free space and can serve to probe strongly correlated matter. In this work, we investigate two-dimensional lattice polarons with strong on-site repulsion and tunable nearest-neighbor interactions using a variational approach including up to one excitation of the medium. We show that extended interactions qualitatively modify the quasiparticle structure beyond the conventional attractive and repulsive polaron picture. A direct analysis of the eigenvalue spectrum reveals the presence of dark impurity states, orthogonal to the bare impurity and therefore spectroscopically dark. These states exhibit nontrivial internal structure, including dipolar symmetries in real space. Our results demonstrate that long-range interactions generate multiple quasiparticle excitations with distinct symmetry properties, highlighting the crucial role of interaction range and lattice geometry. This work opens new avenues for probing hidden quasiparticle states in lattice systems through spectroscopic and wave-function-resolved measurements.
summary: This paper explores how non-local interactions in a 2D lattice change the nature of polarons. It discovers that these interactions create new quasiparticle branches and 'dark' states that cannot be seen in standard spectral measurements due to symmetry. These findings suggest new ways to use quantum microscopy to detect hidden quantum states.
main_problem: Investigating how extended, non-local interactions in a lattice modify the structure of polarons and whether they generate new, spectroscopically invisible 'dark' quasiparticle states.
main_result: The study identifies that nearest-neighbor interactions induce additional repulsive polaron branches and 'dark' impurity states that are orthogonal to the bare impurity, possessing nontrivial dipolar spatial symmetries.
method: A Chevy-type variational approach restricted to a single bosonic excitation, supplemented by a T-matrix approach for benchmarking and symmetry analysis.
model_or_system: A single mobile impurity in a 2D square optical lattice coupled to an ideal Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) at zero temperature, featuring strong on-site repulsion and tunable nearest-neighbor interactions.
key_observables: Energy eigenvalue spectrum, spectral function A(omega), polaron wave functions, and impurity-boson spatial correlations.
important_parameters: On-site repulsion (U0/tb fixed at 12), tunable nearest-neighbor interaction (UI/tb), and lattice geometry (2D square).
main_assumptions: The variational ansatz is limited to at most one bosonic excitation, and the bosonic medium is treated as an ideal gas at zero temperature.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the lattice polaron; Figure 2 displays the two-body scattering spectrum with various bound states; Figure 3 shows the spectral function as a function of frequency and interaction strength; Figure 4 identifies dark states in the energy spectrum; Figure 5 illustrates momentum-space wave functions and real-space spatial correlations; Figure 6 compares the T-matrix and Chevy ansatz spectral functions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of extended interactions, describes the 2D lattice polaron model and variational method, presents two-body scattering results, analyzes the many-body spectrum and the emergence of dark states, discusses spatial symmetries, and concludes with experimental implications.
why_interesting: It reveals 'hidden' many-body states that are invisible to standard spectroscopy but detectable via site-resolved microscopy, providing a new way to probe complex quasiparticle structures in engineered quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of a lattice polaron in a two-dimensional optical lattice. The central blue sphere represents the impurity, while the surrounding green spheres denote the bosonic medium localized on lattice sites. Non-local interactions can lead to dark impurity states.

Figure 2: Scattering states of the two-body problem. The gray points correspond to scattered states. The orange dots correspond to a repulsive bound state from the on-site interaction whereas the black (two degenerate) and red dots correspond to secondary repulsive bound arising from the nonlocal interactions.
Figure 3: Spectral function A(ω) of a lattice polaron for fixed on-site interaction U0 = 12tb, shown as a function of frequency ω/tb and nearest-neighbor interaction UI/tb.
Figure 4: Eigenenergy spectrum obtained from the Chevy ansatz for a lattice polaron with fixed on-site interaction U0 = 12tb, as a function of nearest-neighbor interaction UI/tb.

Figure 5: Momentum-space wave function (top row) impurity-boson spatial corre- lation(bottom row) for the four high-lying polaron states (a)-(b) Correspond to the highest polaron state for UI/tb = 2.0. (c)-(d) show the wave function and impurity- boson spatial correlation for the second highest polaron branch for UI/tb = 2.0. From (e)-(h) we show for two degenerate states featuring a dipole-like structure in real space for or UI/tb = 8.0. These cases correspond to the eigenstates marked by the stars in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4.
